F/U Knee Osteoarthritis, Left evaluated on March 25, 2026
HPI: This is a 66 year old female who:
1. is following up for Knee Osteoarthritis on the left knee. On March 25, 2026, Order Surgery:
Knee was performed. She is here to discuss surgery. Her pain level today is a 6 out of 10,
Left Knee: skin intact, no rashes or lesions.
Left Knee: Normal alignment, no deformity, no
The following images were ordered and obtained:
Additional Images: LT KNEE 3V WB AP, LAT, SUNRISE
Specific Findings: xrays demonstrating severe degenerative joint disease with joint space
Please refer to the education handout for detailed counseling.
Musculoskeletal care: I advised the patient that most patients with degenerative joint
disease of the knee can achieve some measure of pain relief from nonsurgical options like
medications, lifestyle modification, exercise and physical therapy, and use of supportive
devices or change in footwear. The importance of maintaining an ideal body weight or
losing weight to reduce stress on the knee joint is a cornestone of nonoperative
Total knee replacement : I explained to the patient that this is an option for patients that
have severe pain in their knee from arthritis who have failed conservative management.
The diseased parts of the knee are replaced with metal and plastic. Approximately 90% of
patients who have total knee replacement experience a significant reduction in pain and a
dramatic improvement in their ability to perform common activities of daily living. High
impact activities should be avoided after surgery, if possible, to prolong the life of the
implant. Serious complications occur in less than 2% of patients. Risks include but are not
limited to infection, injury to blood vessels and nerves, blood clots, heart complications,
Follow up. Other Instructions: Post-op visit 10-14 dates after surgery date
Electronically Signed By: Arjun Patel, DO, 04/03/2026 08:07 AM PDT
